Question:
The quantities below represent four different transitions between the same initial and final state. Fill in the blanks. \begin{table}[h]
\centering
\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
Q(J) & W(J) & $$\text{Delta} E_{\text{int}} ( \text{J} )$$ \\ \hline
-80 & -120 &  \\ \hline
90 &  &  \\ \hline
 & 40 &  \\ \hline
 & -40 &  \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table} Table 3.2
Answer: Line 1, $$\text{Delta} E_{\text{int}} = 40 \text{J;}$$ line 2, $$W = 50 \text{J}$$ and $$\text{Delta} E_{\text{int}} = 40 \text{J;}$$ line 3, $$Q = 80 \text{J}$$ and $$\text{Delta} E_{\text{int}} = 40 \text{J;}$$ and line 4, $$Q = 0$$ and $$\text{Delta} E_{\text{int}} = 40 \text{J}$$

Question: Why was it necessary to state that the process of Example 3.5 is quasi-static?
Answer: So that the process is represented by the curve $$p = n R T \text{/} V$$ on the pV plot for the evaluation of work.

Question: When 1.00 g of ammonia boils at atmospheric pressure and $$-33.0 \text{°} \text{C,}$$ its volume changes from 1.47 to $$1130 \text{cm}^{3}$$ . Its heat of vaporization at this pressure is $$1.37 \times 10^{6} \text{J/kg} \text{.}$$ What is the change in the internal energy of the ammonia when it vaporizes?
